title: Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture at the Frick Collection
content:
The English artist Thomas Gainsborough is often celebrated as an independent and original genius, especially for his beautiful landscape paintings. However, during his lifetime from 1727 to 1788, it was the highly popular business of portraiture that actually paid his rent. Gainsborough famously despised this 'curs'd face business' because the hours were long and the wealthy clients were incredibly burdensome. He openly joked that wealthy gentlemen had but one part worth looking at, and that was their purse. Furthermore, the tricky question of selecting timeless clothing for these portraits constantly plagued artists of his era.

To handle the complex clothing, most portraitists usually hired a specialist to add the flashing taffetas and wools to their paintings. But unlike most of his contemporaries, Gainsborough was unique because he employed no drapery painter and handled the intricate fabrics himself. This fascinating aspect of his work serves as the springboard for a new exhibition at the Frick Collection titled 'Gainsborough: The Fashion of Portraiture.' Heading into the exhibition, art enthusiasts can expect to see magnificent displays of 18th-century ribbons, laces, and beautiful gowns. The exhibition perfectly highlights his extraordinary ability to capture both the personality of his subjects and the exquisite details of their fashion.
